items.title: Wordgard 0.1 — Marijn Haverbeke starts over, again
items.blurb: The person who built ProseMirror and CodeMirror — the substrate under almost every serious browser editor in existence — has shipped his sixth non-trivial editor, not as a patch on what came before but as a clean break with a new name. He's candid that ProseMirror has parts that make him wince, and that grafting fixes onto it backward-compatibly would produce something 'win32-style.' What's interesting here isn't the software but the posture: someone at the top of a niche choosing to restart rather than calcify.

items.title: Hunting a 16-year-old SQLite bug with TLA+
items.blurb: A write-ahead log bug sat undetected in SQLite — one of the most audited pieces of software on earth — from 2010 until 2026, then was finally cornered using TLA+ formal verification as a detective tool rather than a proof tool. The Canonical team used it to model the exact sequence of states that produce database corruption, then built a second model of how their own dqlite layer uses SQLite to check whether the corruption path is reachable from above. It's a case study in how formal methods become useful not for guaranteeing correctness but for making failure legible.

items.title: Making old blood stem cells young again — the lysosome angle
items.blurb: Mount Sinai researchers identified overactive, damaged lysosomes — the cell's internal recycling centers — as a primary driver of blood stem cell aging in mice, not a downstream symptom but a causal mechanism. By calming lysosomal overactivity, they restored the stem cells' ability to regenerate balanced blood and immune cells at youthful levels. This is early-stage mouse work, but the mechanistic specificity is what makes it notable: it points to a defined intervention target rather than a vague 'aging pathway.'

items.title: Elecoglipron — the oral GLP-1 pill that actually worked in Phase 2b
items.blurb: The SOLSTICE trial, published simultaneously in The Lancet and presented at ADA Scientific Sessions, showed that elecoglipron — an oral GLP-1 receptor agonist — significantly lowered blood glucose and reduced body weight in people with type 2 diabetes. Oral delivery is the key unlock: the injectable GLP-1 market is massive, but needle-aversion and cold-chain logistics lock out a large share of potential patients. A pill that delivers comparable results changes the access geometry of the entire drug class.

sections.heading: Making as politics, resistance as aesthetic

items.title: Summer of Ludd — Gen Z's anti-tech festival is happening in Tompkins Square Park right now
items.blurb: A weeklong festival in the East Village — papier-mâché puppets, a small orchestra in Pride regalia, zines on getting off Spotify and surveillance in schools, workshops on flirting offline — is staging the actual Luddite history (artisans resisting industrial displacement, met with state violence) as a live organizing frame for 2026. The interesting part isn't the phone-free posturing; it's that the original Luddites weren't anti-technology, they were anti-the-specific-use-of-technology-to-destroy-their-livelihoods, and this crowd seems to know that. A scene forming in real time, a few blocks from you.

items.title: Ben Krasnow: AFM high-speed video, selective stainless steel etching, diffraction gratings
items.blurb: Ben Krasnow went unreasonably deep on two things at once: high-speed atomic force microscope video (imaging at the nanoscale fast enough to watch dynamic processes) and selective chemical etching of stainless steel to fabricate diffraction gratings. Applied Science is the benchmark for 'one person, one problem, no shortcuts' — the channel where craft and physics meet with no audience-building concessions.

items.title: The world's tallest tropical trees have no hydraulic ceiling — the assumption was wrong
items.blurb: For decades the working model was that as trees grow taller, gravity and vessel length create a hydraulic ceiling that limits water transport, photosynthesis, and growth — an elegant constraint that explained why trees don't just keep getting taller. A new study in Science, led by Exeter and Cardiff, found that giant Dipterocarp trees (some exceeding 80 meters) have evolved vessel architectures that fully compensate for height, leaving them no more drought-vulnerable than short trees of the same species. The infrastructure was there, quietly working, and we'd been modeling it wrong the whole time.

facts.fact: SQLite's write-ahead log bug that was just fixed had been present in the codebase since 2010 — 16 years — in one of the most widely deployed and audited pieces of software in existence.

Meduza journalist Galina Timchenko's iPhone was infected with Pegasus spyware while she was in Berlin — making her the first documented case of a Pegasus infection of a Russian journalist — exactly two weeks after Russia declared Meduza an 'undesirable organization.'

Giant Dipterocarp trees exceeding 80 meters showed no height-related loss in growth during a severe drought compared to smaller trees of the same species, overturning a widely accepted hydraulic limit theory.

Marijn Haverbeke has now built six non-trivial text editors, including ProseMirror and CodeMirror, and chose to release his latest as a completely new project rather than a ProseMirror 2.0 to avoid ambiguity in the ecosystem.

The FDA's Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee will review BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-C, Semax, and several other peptides at its July 23–24, 2026 meeting — a regulatory moment that will shape the legal compounding landscape for the entire peptide field.

art_picks.title: Koyaanisqatsi
art_picks.creator: Godfrey Reggio
art_picks.why: A film with no characters and no plot — just systems made visible: industry, sprawl, time, speed, and the feedback loops between humans and infrastructure. Glass's score turns it into pure frequency; it will rewire how you see a city block.

art_picks.title: Vera Molnár — Works
art_picks.creator: Vera Molnár
art_picks.why: The mother of generative art, working with algorithms and plotters from the 1960s before most people had seen a computer. Her whole practice is the question you live in: what does expressive mean when the author is a rule? The obsessive variation series are direct ancestors of any generative 3D work you make.

art_picks.title: Big Science
art_picks.creator: Laurie Anderson
art_picks.why: The 1982 album that turned technology, mythology, and American cultural anxiety into a single strange voice-processed object. Anderson was doing performance-as-systems-art before the vocabulary existed — it still sounds like a transmission from just slightly outside now.

events.name: Greater New York 2026
events.why: PS1's sixth-edition survey of 50+ NYC-area artists in the formative years of their careers — the closest thing New York has to a live map of where practice is actually forming right now, before it hardens into market positions.

events.name: Yves Saint Laurent and Photography
events.venue: International Center of Photography (ICP), 84 Ludlow Street, New York
events.why: ~300 objects tracing how photography became the actual engine of a fashion mythology — not documentation but identity construction, with Avedon, Newton, Penn, Warhol as co-authors of a brand-as-cultural-symbol.

events.name: EGEVÆRK: Forms of Belonging
events.why: A collaborative exhibition and live residency by Danish designers Mette Bentzen and Lasse Kristensen — hand-carved furniture as landscape meditation; obsessive craft with deep material intelligence at the physical/sculptural edge.

events.name: Gabriel Orozco: Public Nature
events.venue: JCDecaux Bus Shelters, New York City (citywide)
events.why: Orozco's first commissioned photographic series — 10 new site-specific images of urban life embedded into the city's own infrastructure, blurring the line between art, object, and found environment.

events.name: Celebrate Brooklyn! — Royel Otis (Benefit Concert)
events.venue: Lena Horne Bandshell, Prospect Park, Brooklyn
events.why: The 47th season of Celebrate Brooklyn runs under a 'Radical Joy' theme — Royel Otis brings their wry, textured indie pop to one of the city's best outdoor stages on a warm July night.

digest.intro: Things built by hand, things broken by time, things that quietly rewired themselves.
